Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend To File First Amended Complaint; And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
Off Calendar Feb-11-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DENNIS HYUN, Esq. of Hyun Legal, APC (tel: 213-488-6555) for Plaintiff Marcus Nunes.
Atty. MICHAEL SIGALL, Esq. and Atty. ELIZABETH LEVY, Esq. of Seyfarth Shaw LLP (tel: 310-277-7200) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF MARCUS NUNES, AS A PROXY FOR THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Leave To Amend To File First Amended Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to amend is denied. The Hyun declaration does not comply with CRC 3.1324, which requires, inter alia that a declaration in support of the motion to amend state "[w]hen the facts giving rise to the amended allegations were discovered; and . . . [t]he reasons why the request for amendment was not made earlier."

Second, plaintiff unreasonably delayed making this application. At the latest, plaintiff had all of the facts it needed to plead these new claims a year ago when the court declared the arbitration clause unenforceable. (See Reply, 4:14-16; Melican v. Regents of University of California (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 168, 176 [denying amendment where plaintiff gave no explanation for the delay]; Record v. Reason (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 472, 486 ["[E]ven if a good amendment is proposed in proper form, unwarranted delay in presenting it may-of itself-be a valid reason for denial."]; Leader v. Health Industries of America, Inc. (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 603, 613 [it "is well settled that a long deferred presentation of the proposed amendment without a showing of excuse for the delay is itself a significant factor to uphold the trial court's denial of the amendment"].)

The court further finds defendant's argument about prejudice well taken; plaintiff's proposed amendment would transform this case from a simple PAGA representative/individual action to a class action.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Ryan Wheeler, CSR# 13717 (tel: 760-805-3979 / email: ryanwheeler91@yahoo.com) (301/CVA)

Notice Of Renewed Motion And Renewed Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, In The Alternative, Request For Reconsideration
Off Calendar Feb-11-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DENNIS HYUN, Esq. of Hyun Legal, APC (tel: 213-488-6555) for Plaintiff Marcus Nunes.
Atty. MICHAEL SIGALL, Esq. and Atty. ELIZABETH LEVY, Esq. of Seyfarth Shaw LLP (tel: 310-277-7200) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS MICHAEL KORS STORES (CALIFORNIA), INC., AND MICHAEL KORS STORES (CALIFORNIA), LLC's Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, In The Alternative, Request For Reconsideration.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's renewed motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied.

"A party filing a renewed application must, among other things, submit an affidavit showing what 'new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed' (id., subd. (b)) to justify the renewed application, and show diligence with a satisfactory explanation for not presenting the new or different information earlier (California Correctional Peace Officers Assn. v. Virga (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 30, 45-46, 103 Cal.Rptr.3d 699, & fns. 14-15; 826 see Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 688-690, 68 Cal.Rptr.2d 228)." (Even Zohar Construction & Remodeling, Inc. v. Bellaire Townhouses, LLC (2015) 61 Cal.4th 830, 833.)

Here, Defendant contends that Ramirez v. Charter Communications, (2024) 16 Cal.5th 478, is new law supporting reconsideration of the court's February 1, 2024 order denying its prior motion to compel arbitration. That order rested on the court's conclusion that the arbitration agreement is "permeated with unconscionability." (Defendant's RJN, Ex. 1, at p. 3.) Ramirez provided additional guidance to courts on applying the severability analysis of Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc., (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 121. (See Ramirez, 16 Cal.5th, at pp. 513-518.) Ramirez disapproved certain bright-line rules about severability adopted by some courts. (Id. at p. 516.) It is not clear to this court that Ramirez provides a change of law that can justify a reconsideration motion, or that the prior decision of this court relied on any reasoning Ramirez disapproved, but in any event, even assuming that a reconsideration motion is justifiable here, defendant fails to demonstrate diligence. The California Supreme Court issued Ramirez on July 15, 2024, and defendant filed this motion on January 15, 2025. The court notes that defendant could have filed this motion months ago, but instead it acted inconsistently with the right to compel arbitration and filed a motion for judgment on the pleadings on September 20, 2024. In any event, the Sigall declaration in support of defendant's motion does not include all of the information required by CCP 1008(b) ["shall be shown by affidavit what application was made before, when and to what judge, what order or decisions were made, and what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown"].

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Ryan Wheeler, CSR# 13717 (tel: 760-805-3979 / email: ryanwheeler91@yahoo.com) (301/CVA)
